---
title: "Story of Science"
description-meta: "Stories in science that inspired me"
author: "Gang He"
categories: [story]
date: "2026-02-08"
title-block-banner: false
page-layout: full
from: markdown+emoji
---

<!--This is my personal clarity, please delete or replace with your own clarity-->
{{< include /files/includes/_msclarity.qmd >}}


Science is often described as a sequence of discoveries, but the human stories behind those discoveries are just as important. Many breakthroughs began with stubborn curiosity, imperfect tools, long periods of doubt, and people who were willing to follow a strange result when others would have ignored it.

These stories remind me that science is not only about being right early. It is about paying attention, building evidence patiently, and letting reality correct our assumptions.

## Ronald J. Konopka and the *Period* Gene

In the early 1970s, Ronald J. Konopka was a graduate student in Seymour Benzer's lab at Caltech. He wanted to know whether behavior could be traced to genes. At the time, this was a risky question. Genes were already central to biology, but linking a single gene to a complex behavior like daily rhythm sounded almost too ambitious.

Konopka used fruit flies and searched for mutants with abnormal circadian rhythms. After screening many flies, he found three remarkable types: one with no regular rhythm, one with a shorter-than-normal rhythm, and one with a longer-than-normal rhythm. All three mapped to the same genetic region. That gene became known as *period*.

The result changed biology. It showed that a daily biological clock was not just a vague physiological tendency. It had a genetic basis. Decades later, work by Jeffrey C. Hall, Michael Rosbash, and Michael W. Young explained how the molecular clock works, earning them the 2017 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

Konopka's story inspires science because it shows the power of asking a precise question about a messy phenomenon. If behavior seems too complex, the answer is not to avoid it. The answer is to design a clever system where complexity becomes measurable.

## Jeffrey C. Hall

Jeffrey C. Hall helped turn Konopka's genetic clue into a molecular story. Working with Michael Rosbash at Brandeis University, Hall studied how the *period* gene affected the daily rhythm of fruit flies. Their work helped reveal that the PER protein rises and falls over the course of a day, creating a feedback loop that keeps biological time.

Hall's path was not a simple story of institutional comfort. He was known for being intense, blunt, and deeply committed to the details of experimental work. Later in his career, he became frustrated with the pressure, funding culture, and bureaucracy of academic science. He left research before the Nobel Prize came.

That part of the story matters. Science depends on people, and people are not machines for producing papers. Hall's experience reminds us that great science often comes from obsession with the problem, but the scientific system also has to protect the people doing that work.

His story inspires science by showing that details matter. The daily clock was not solved by one beautiful idea alone. It took careful genetics, behavior assays, molecular biology, and the willingness to connect small observations into a mechanism.

## Michael Rosbash

Michael Rosbash worked with Hall to understand the molecular rhythm of the *period* gene. Their group showed that *period* RNA and protein levels oscillate during the day. This suggested a feedback loop: the gene produces a protein, the protein builds up, and then it helps shut down its own production. After the protein degrades, the cycle begins again.

That mechanism seems elegant now, but it was not obvious at the beginning. The scientists had to move between behavior, genetics, RNA, protein, and timing. The difficulty was not only finding the parts, but proving how the parts formed a clock.

Rosbash's story inspires science because it shows how mechanism grows from persistence. A biological rhythm looks mysterious when observed from far away. Once scientists measure the right variables at the right times, the mystery becomes a system.

## Michael W. Young

Michael W. Young, working independently at Rockefeller University, found additional genes that made the clock understandable. His lab identified *timeless* and later helped uncover other key components of the circadian system. These discoveries showed that *period* was part of a larger molecular circuit rather than a lone actor.

The independent confirmation was crucial. Hall and Rosbash's work could have remained one strong model from one research lineage. Young's discoveries made the field stronger by revealing parallel evidence and missing pieces.

Young's story inspires science because it shows why independent lines of evidence matter. Science becomes reliable when different groups, using different routes, converge on the same structure of reality.

## Rosalind Franklin

Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction work was essential to understanding the structure of DNA. Her famous "Photo 51" showed a pattern that strongly supported a helical structure. James Watson and Francis Crick used crucial information from Franklin's work when building their DNA model, though Franklin herself did not receive the recognition she deserved during her lifetime.

Franklin was not only "the woman behind DNA." That framing is too small. She was an exceptional physical chemist with a disciplined eye for data. Before DNA, she studied the structure of coal and carbon. After DNA, she made important contributions to the study of viruses, including tobacco mosaic virus and polio virus.

Her story inspires science in two ways. First, it shows that beautiful theories depend on rigorous measurements. Second, it warns us that credit in science is not automatically fair. A scientific community that wants truth must also care about how evidence, authorship, and recognition are handled.

## Yitang (Tom) Zhang

Yitang Zhang's story is one of the most striking examples of delayed recognition in modern mathematics. After earning his PhD, he spent years in unstable positions and outside the usual prestige track. He worked at a Subway restaurant for a period, took teaching jobs, and continued thinking about deep mathematical problems with little public attention.

In 2013, Zhang shocked the mathematical world by proving that there are infinitely many pairs of prime numbers separated by a bounded gap. He did not prove the full twin prime conjecture, which predicts infinitely many prime pairs separated by 2, but he proved something that had resisted mathematicians for generations: some finite bound exists.

What made the result powerful was not only the theorem itself. It was the path. Zhang kept working on a problem when there was no obvious career reward for doing so. He combined patience, technical mastery, and a willingness to stay with a hard question for years.

Zhang's story inspires science because it reminds us that the best work is not always produced by the most visible person in the most famous institution. Sometimes the key ingredient is a mind that refuses to stop thinking.

## Barbara McClintock

Barbara McClintock discovered transposable elements, often called "jumping genes," through her work on maize. Her results showed that genes were not fixed beads on a string. Some genetic elements could move and regulate the expression of other genes.

For years, many scientists did not fully accept the significance of her work. The conceptual world of genetics was not ready for what she saw. McClintock continued to trust careful observation over fashionable assumptions, and decades later her work became central to modern genetics. She received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1983.

McClintock's story inspires science because it shows that seeing clearly can be lonely. When the data are strong but the field is not ready, patience becomes part of the method.

## Tu Youyou

Tu Youyou led the discovery of artemisinin, one of the most important antimalarial drugs in history. Her team searched ancient Chinese medical texts, tested many plant extracts, and eventually identified a low-temperature extraction method for *Artemisia annua* that preserved the active compound.

This was not a simple case of tradition replacing science. It was tradition being interrogated by experiment. Historical knowledge suggested where to look, and modern pharmacology determined what actually worked.

Tu's story inspires science because it shows that useful knowledge can come from unexpected archives. The scientific move is not to romanticize old knowledge or dismiss it. The scientific move is to test it carefully.

## Katalin Kariko

Katalin Kariko spent decades working on mRNA technology when the field was unfashionable and funding was difficult. She faced repeated setbacks, demotions, and skepticism. With Drew Weissman, she helped solve a key problem: how to modify mRNA so that it could be used therapeutically without provoking destructive immune responses.

That work became foundational for mRNA vaccines and opened a wider field of RNA-based medicine. The overnight success was not overnight at all. It was the visible result of decades of persistence.

Kariko's story inspires science because it shows that a field can look impractical right before it becomes transformative. Some ideas need better tools, better timing, and researchers stubborn enough to keep them alive.

## What These Stories Share

These scientists worked in different fields, but their stories share a pattern:

- They noticed something that did not fit the existing picture.
- They made the vague measurable: behavior, DNA structure, prime gaps, gene movement, traditional remedies, or RNA therapy.
- They stayed with hard problems longer than seemed reasonable.
- They show that science advances through both imagination and discipline.

The lesson is not simply "be persistent." Persistence without evidence is just stubbornness. The deeper lesson is to build a life of attention: notice anomalies, respect data, design sharp tests, and keep enough courage to follow the result.

<!--Include social share buttons-->

{{< include /files/includes/_socialshare.qmd >}}
